title: Document Room Condition
summary: Record the condition of a room with notes and photos when moving in or out.
learnerPromise: You will be able to create a clear record of a room's condition to protect yourself from unfair charges and support honest conversations with landlords or roommates.
whyItMatters: A move-in or move-out record helps prevent disputes about damage, security deposits, and who is responsible for repairs. Photos and notes are much more reliable than memory alone.
realLifeUses: Photograph and note every wall, floor, and appliance before moving into a rental
Record the condition of a dorm room at the start and end of a school year
Document wear and tear to show it existed before you moved in
Share a condition report with a landlord to set clear expectations
result: A dated set of room-by-room notes and photos showing the condition of walls, floors, fixtures, and appliances.
youWillLearn: Systematically check each area of a room for existing damage or wear
Take clear, dated photos that show the full context of any issue
Write short, factual notes that describe what you see
Store the documentation in a place where you can find it later
miniChallenge: Pick one room and take five photos of areas that might show wear or damage. Write one sentence about each photo.
steps: Walk into the room and start at the door, moving clockwise around the space
Photograph each wall, the floor, the ceiling, windows, and any fixtures or appliances
Write a short note for each photo: what it shows and the date
Open cabinets, turn on lights, and test outlets to check if things work
Save all photos and notes together with the date and room name in the filename or folder
completionCriteria: You have at least five dated photos of different parts of the room
Each photo has a short written note describing what it shows
commonProblems: Poor lighting makes damage hard to see in photos
It is easy to forget to check less obvious spots like inside drawers or behind doors
Some issues only show up after you have lived there for a while
tips: Take photos in daylight or with the room lights on for the clearest images
Include a wide shot first, then a close-up of any damage for context
If you are moving in, try to do this before you bring in furniture
Send a copy to your landlord or roommate so everyone has the same record
